Hallucinations.text: The FBI charged Keonna Thomas, 30, from Philadelphia on Thursday for attempting to travel overseas to join ISIS. She is one of three women arrested this week on terror charges

Two other women, Noelle Velentzas, 28, and Asia Siddiqui, 31, were arrested in New York for planning to build an explosive device for attacks in the US. Thomas purchased an electronic visa to Turkey on March 23 and was charged with knowingly attempting to provide material support and resources to a designated foreign terrorist organization. She could face 15 years in prison. The Justice Department's National Security Division has prosecuted or is prosecuting over 30 cases of people attempting to travel abroad to join or support terrorist groups in the past 18 months, with 18 of those cases involving support to ISIS. Homeland Security Secretary Jeh Johnson stated that the terrorist threat is more decentralized, more diffuse, and more complicated, involving potential lone wolf actors and effective use of social media and the Internet.

Blue Bell Ice Cream has temporarily shut down its manufacturing plant in Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, due to the discovery of listeria contamination in a serving of ice cream produced there. The Centers for Disease

and Prevention (CDC) has warned consumers not to consume Blue Bell-branded products made at this plant with the letters O, P, Q, R, S or T behind the coding date. This warning does not affect other Blue Bell ice cream not made at the Broken Arrow plant. Blue Bell is shutting down the facility "out of an abundance of caution" to search for the source of contamination. This is the third time Blue Bell has taken action due to a listeria outbreak at a hospital in Kansas that served the company's ice cream. The CDC advises individuals and institutions to check their freezers for the recalled products and discard them. Listeriosis is a serious infection caused by consuming food contaminated with listeria, primarily affecting the elderly, pregnant women, newborns, and people with weakened immune systems. Symptoms include fever and muscle aches, sometimes with diarrhea or other gastrointestinal symptoms. In the United States, approximately 1,600 people become seriously ill each year, and 16% of these cases result in death. Pregnant women may experience stillbirth or spontaneous abortion during the second

information provided, Phi Kappa Psi and other fraternities on the University of Virginia campus may have a difficult time suing Rolling Stone for defamation due to the nature of defamation law and the specific circumstances of the case. The university cannot sue for defamation as it is a governmental entity and cannot bring such a cause of action. Phi Kappa Psi, as a group, may have a harder time establishing that the defamatory statements were "of or concerning" individual members, making it difficult for them to bring a successful lawsuit. However, there are exceptions to this rule, and Phi Kappa Psi may argue that the "small group" exception applies to them, as the defamatory statements have been imputed directly to individual members, who have suffered harm by their association with the group.

She believes Paul's message of inclusion is hollow, as his policies do not align with the needs of minority communities. The author highlights Paul's opposition to the Civil Rights Act, the Department of Education, and protections for DREAMers, among other issues. She also criticizes Paul's lack of cultural competency and condescending attitude towards historically black colleges and universities. The author
by stating that America needs a leader who respects the intelligence of the American people and works to expand democracy, not dismantle it,

Mary Kay Letourneau Fualaau and her husband, Vili Fualaau, will share their story in an exclusive interview with ABC's "20/20" on May 14, 2021. The couple, who have been married for ten years, met when Mary Kay was a 34-year-old teacher and Vili was her 13-year-old student. They had a child together and both served time in prison for their relationship. In the interview, Mary Kay will discuss what keeps their marriage strong despite their significant age difference, her plans to teach again, and her intention to have her sex offender status lifted. Vili will talk about his struggles with alcoholism, depression, and his belief that the system failed him as a minor. Mary Kay is now 53, and Vili is 31.

Kanye West and the paparazzi photographer, Daniel Ramos, have reached a settlement in the lawsuit filed after West assaulted Ramos at Los Angeles International Airport in 2013. The details of the settlement, including the amount of damages, were not disclosed, but Ramos' lawyer, Gloria Allred, confirmed that West apologized to Ramos as part of the agreement. The case was set for trial next week, but has been dismissed. West pleaded no contest to a misdemeanor count of battery in the incident last year and was sentenced to probation, anger management sessions, and community service. The original incident was captured on video and showed West attempting to take the photographer's camera. Ramos' lawyer issued a statement saying, "We believe that this case sent an important message. Celebrities are not above the law, and they have no right to physically attack someone simply because they were asked a question." In
a civil settlement after being accused of assaulting a man at a Beverly Hills chiropractor's office.
B.B. King, the blues legend also known as "Blues Boy," was hospitalized due to dehydration caused by his Type II diabetes. However
he has since been discharged from the hospital and is currently resting at home

King, who is 89 years old, released a statement expressing his gratitude for the concerns of his fans. He is best known for his hits "The Thrill Is Gone," "There Must be a Better World Somewhere," and "When Love Comes to Town," and has used custom-made Gibson guitars named Lucille throughout his career. King was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1987 and has received 30 Grammy nominations. He cancelled the remainder of his tour last year due to dehydration and exhaustion.

Chris Copeland of the Indiana Pacers was stabbed in front of the Fulton Houses project down the street from 1OAK nightclub in New York City early Wednesday. The suspect, who never entered the club, is in police custody, and charges against him are pending

Two Atlanta Hawks, Pero Antic and Thabo Sefolosha, were arrested on charges of obstructing governmental administration and disorderly conduct. They went outside to check on Copeland after hearing about the incident and were arrested while trying to push through the crowd. Copeland suffered a knife wound to his left elbow and abdomen and is in stable condition at a New York hospital. Copeland and his companion, Katrine Saltara, were in the club for about 10 minutes before leaving and being stabbed.

Selena's family is creating a hologram-like figure of the Tejano star, which they call "Selena the One." This digital embodiment of Selena will be able to walk, talk, sing, and dance. The technology is being handled by Acrovirt LLC and is described as a "Detailed individual personalized function spanning the mind, brain and body, the individual's Digitized Human Essence will autonomously learn and react on behalf of its human counterpart's." Selena's sister, Suzette Quintanilla, expressed her excitement about Selena being the first figure to use this technology and the fact that she is a Latina. The project aims to release new songs and videos, collaborate with current artists, and go on tour in 2018. Fans can support the launch through an Indiegogo campaign starting April 16. The commemorative Fiesta de la Flor in Corpus Christi, Texas, celebrating Selena's life, is scheduled for April 17 and 18.

The ramp agent from Menzies Aviation, who was caught napping in the cargo hold of Alaska Airlines Flight 448, reportedly fell asleep during his shift and caused the plane to make an emergency landing due to the banging noises he made. The cargo hold is pressurized and temperature-controlled, and the man was not in any immediate danger. Passengers on the plane heard the banging and screaming for help, and a federal air marshal intervened. The ramp agent was taken to a hospital as a precaution and passed a drug test before being released. The incident is under investigation,
All ramp employees undergo criminal background checks before being hired and have security badges. The flight with 170 passengers and six crew members arrived in Los Angeles later that day.

Emy Afalava, a decorated American veteran born in American Samoa, has been denied the right to vote due to the federal government's insistence that he is not a citizen, despite being subject to American law his whole life. This situation contradicts the Fourteenth Amendment, which declares that persons born in the United States and subject to its jurisdiction are citizens
Afalava and other American Samoans are currently seeking to right this injustice in a federal lawsuit before the D.C. Circuit.

Historically, the United States has recognized that those born on America's lands are citizens. However, there have been exceptions, such as the infamous Dred Scott case in 1857, which legitimized slavery and denied citizenship to free African-Americans. This decision is now widely regarded as one of the worst in Supreme Court history.

Another example is the case of Isabel Gonzalez, a Puerto Rican woman who was prevented from entering the mainland in 1904 because officials considered her an undesirable alien, despite her belief that she was a U.S. citizen due to Puerto Rico's status as a U.S. territory. The Supreme Court declined to decide whether Gonzalez was a citizen, citing fears of racial and social issues. This ruling allowed federal officials to deny Gonzalez and others full citizenship rights for years

The sisters Iman and Siham, collectively known as Faarrow, are currently working on their debut album in Los Angeles. Their musical influences include Michael Jackson, Lionel Richie, the Spice Girls, Mariah Carey, Whitney Houston, Sia, and LunchMoney Lewis. They have been working with producer Elijah Kelley, who has brought their Afro-pop sound to life with a fusion of pop, hip hop, and African percussion. The entire album was written and produced by the sisters, Iman, Si

, and Elijah. In their free time, they work with the U.N. High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) as spokespersons, advocating for refugees and headlining events such as World Refugee Day at the Kennedy Center and the Nansen Awards in Geneva. They have also started a socially conscious brand called "Wish Creatively," selling bracelets and necklaces where the proceeds go back to projects in Kenya and Somalia. Next, they are in the mixing process for their album and plan to release the first single as soon as possible. Iman and Siham feel inspired to use their creative platform to inspire people and give back to their homeland of Somalia.

Senate passed a Medicare reform bill on Tuesday with a bipartisan vote of 92 to 8, including a permanent solution to the "doc fix" and a two-year extension of the Children's Health Insurance Program. The bill, which also avoids cuts to physicians' payments, is expected to be signed by President Barack Obama

Conservative senators, such as Ted Cruz and Marco Rubio, proposed amendments to offset the more than $200 billion cost, but they were defeated. House Speaker John Boehner urged the Senate to pass the House-passed bill without changes. Cruz and Rubio voted against the bill, while Rand Paul voted for it.

The trailer for "Batman v. Superman: Dawn of Justice" was leaked online and featured commentary about Superman, footage of Batman's appearance, and the two superheroes facing each other. It was set to premiere in Imax theaters on Monday, March 25, 2016. The film stars Ben Affleck as Batman, Henry Cavill as Superman, and Amy Adams. The cost of getting Mark Hamill's autograph at "Star Wars" celebration is not mentioned in the article.
and the small sovereign state of Vatican City, faces

balancing spiritual and worldly concerns. This was evident on Sunday when Pope Francis had to decide whether to use the term "genocide" to describe the deaths of over a million Armenians a century ago at the hands of the Turks. Previous popes had avoided using the term, but Francis quoted John Paul II's description of it as "the first genocide of the 20th century" during a Mass commemorating the tragedy. This decision maintained the Vatican's concern for continuity while acknowledging the historical significance of the event. However, Turkey was not pleased and recalled its ambassador for consultations and summoned the Vatican's ambassador for a meeting. Turkey's Foreign Minister Mevlut Cavusoglu called the Pope's use of the term "unacceptable" and "out of touch with both historical facts and legal basis." Despite the controversy, Francis continues to engage in geopolitics with his unique blend of diplomacy and bluntness.
During National Park Week from April 18 to 26, 128 fee-charging national parks in the United States
including Yellowstone and Yosemite, will offer free admission on Saturdays and Sundays. This initiative is a collaboration between the
Park Service and the National Park Foundation

Visitors can enjoy various activities such as night-time astronomy parties, daytime historical programs, Earth Day celebrations, and family-friendly Junior Ranger activities. To discover park sites near you and learn more, visit FindYourPark.com. Share

The second robotic probe sent into the Fukushima nuclear plant captured images of a strange green glow. TEPCO reported that the robot detected lower radiation levels and temperature than expected, indicating effective cooling systems. Akira Ono, the chief of Fukushima Daiichi plant, stated, "It is a great step forward towards the decommissioning work as we can earn necessary data for the next investigation." The cause of the yellow discoloration and the green glow is unknown. TEPCO sent in the shape-shifting robots to assess damage in one of the reactors that suffered a meltdown after the 2011 earthquake and tsunami. The site's radiation levels are still too high for workers to enter, and decommissioning work is estimated to cost $50 billion and take years to complete. TEPCO called the robotic probe an "unprecedented" experiment.
Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, is facing the victim impact part of his trial

politician Nigel Farage for a duel in Hyde Park over Farage's immigration policy. Farage, who is the leader of the UK Independence Party (UKIP), has criticized Britain's membership in the European Union for allowing a large influx of immigrants from Eastern Europe. Seven people, including Illinois State University associate men's basketball coach Torrey Ward and deputy athletic director Aaron Leetch, tragically died in a plane crash about 2 miles east of the Central Illinois Regional Airport in Bloomington. The small plane, a Cessna 414, was coming back from the NCAA Final Four championship game in Indianapolis. Ward and Leetch were well-respected and much-loved colleagues in the Illinois State athletics department. Ward, a standout player at the University of Alabama at Birmingham, had a coaching career that included stops at Jacksonville State University, the University of Mississippi, and China before joining Illinois State in 2012. Leetch, who came from Arkansas State University in 2005, served various roles at Illinois State before leaving to serve as director of athletics at Whitworth University in Spokane, Washington, and returning in 2013 as ISU's deputy director of athletics. The Illinois State community is deeply grieving the loss of Ward and Leetch.
The survivors and family members of those killed in the Boston Marathon bombing testified in the sentencing phase of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev's trial on Thursday

Steve Woolfenden, a biomedical researcher, described how he was holding his son's stroller handles when the bomb went off, and how he later noticed his own severe injuries, including a torn Achilles tendon and a protruding left tibia. Witnesses also testified about the traumatic experiences they had during the bombing, including Heather Abbott, who lost her foot, and Marc Fucarile, who suffered severe burns and may need to have his left leg amputated above the knee. Dr. David King, a trauma surgeon, described the carnage he saw at the scene and the difficult decisions some victims had to make regarding their injuries. One of the most emotional moments came when witnesses testified about the death of Martin Richard, an 8-year-old boy who was only a few feet from the bomb and died of rapid blood loss and internal injuries. The defense is expected to begin calling witnesses on Monday to try to soften Tsarnaev's portrayal as cold-hearted and remorseless. The jury must decide whether Tsarnaev will receive the death penalty or spend the rest of his life in prison.

According to the article from CNN, Sofia Vergara's ex-fiance, Nick Loeb, has filed a lawsuit in Los Angeles to prevent Vergara from destroying their two frozen embryos created through in vitro fertilization in 2013. The embryos are currently being held at the Art Reproductive Center in Beverly Hills. Loeb wants to implant the embryos in a surrogate and bring them to term, but he doesn't want any financial responsibilities or obligations from Vergara. The lawsuit also names the clinic as a defendant and asks the court to prevent the embryos from being destroyed until both parties reach an agreement. The case has raised questions about who has the right to embryos and when life begins. Fertility specialist Dr. David Tourgeman explained that typically, the mother or the origin of the egg is given power of attorney over the embryos, but if there's a disagreement, the courts usually get involved to decide who legally owns the embryos. California attorney Vance Owen added that this lawsuit is bringing up interesting issues, including religious, moral, legal, and contract issues, and a California court will have to decide to whom the embryos belong. which resulted in three deaths and over 260 injuries, including the death of an 8-year-old boy named Martin Richard. 1. The jury consisted of 12 members, including a foreperson, who deliberated for more than 35 hours over 7 days in the Massachusetts murder trial of former NFL star Aaron Hernandez.

2. They listened to the testimony of over 130 witnesses and reviewed over 400 pieces of evidence in the case of the 2013 shooting death of Hernandez's friend Odin Lloyd.
3. The jury found Hernandez guilty of first-degree murder and he was sentenced to life without parole in a maximum-security prison.

4. Jurors were struck by the viciousness of multiple gunshots and the indifference shown by Hernandez towards his friend's life.

Larry Johnson described the fear and helplessness he felt during the emergency landing of SkyWest Flight 5622 in Buffalo, New York. He recalled a flight attendant asking for medical assistance due to a sick passenger, followed by an announcement of a pressurization problem and the need for passengers to prepare for an emergency landing

Johnson and his family, including his 8-month-old son, experienced symptoms such as dizziness, wooziness, and difficulty breathing. Three passengers reportedly lost consciousness during the flight, and fifteen were examined by medical personnel at the airport, but no one was hospitalized. SkyWest spokeswoman Marissa Snow stated that maintenance personnel found no indication of a pressurization problem with the Embraer E170 aircraft, and the airline continues to investigate the cause. The National Transportation Safety Board is also gathering information on the incident. Nurse Mary Cunningham, who was on board and attended to the first sick passenger, described the situation as affecting oxygen in a specific area of the plane. There were 84 passengers and nine crew members on board,

the plane descended 28,000 feet in three minutes. CNN aviation analyst Mary Schiavo described the descent as feeling like a roller coaster, and Johnson expressed relief that the experience ended safely.

Despite the lack of remorse or plausible alibi from Hernandez during the trial, the author argues that his arrogance and swagger were the biggest crimes in the eyes of many. However, the focus should be on the evidence, which led to the guilty verdict. Hernandez was also found guilty of unlawful possession of a firearm and ammunition. The second mate of the Houston Express was surprised to find a man signaling for help in a small boat hundreds of miles from land. The man, Louis Jordan, had been drifting on his 35-foot Pearson sailboat for over two months after a storm capsized it and broke his mast. He was able to rig a makeshift mast and sail, but progress was slow due to strong currents and numerous storms

Jordan survived by collecting rainwater and catching fish that were attracted to his laundry. He was rescued on Thursday and was in surprisingly good condition upon arrival at the hospital. Coast Guard officials have confirmed Jordan's story and noted that his father reported him missing on January 29. Jordan is an unemployed truck driver who lived on his boat at a marina in Conway, South Carolina, but set out to sea in search of bigger fish when it became difficult to catch dinner. His father, Frank Jordan, believes his son's strong constitution and faith sustained him during his ordeal. Jordan is expected to return to the Bucksport Plantation Marina & RV Resort next week.

The French prosecutor, Brice Robin, stated that no video footage from on board Germanwings Flight 9525 has been used in the investigation. Reports of a cell phone video showing the final moments of the flight are false. Co-pilot Andreas Lubitz had a history of depression, but there is no evidence suggesting he was suicidal or acting aggressively before the crash. Investigators are looking into whether Lubitz feared losing his pilot's license due to his medical condition. No visible human remains have been found at the crash site, but recovery teams are continuing their search. French President Francois Hollande expects all victims to be identified using DNA analysis by the end of the week. The recovery of victims' personal belongings, including cell phones, will begin on Wednesday.

Robert Lewis Burns Jr., one of the original drummers in the Southern rock band Lynyrd Skynyrd, passed away in a car crash on Friday night. The incident occurred in Cartersville, Georgia, and involved only Burns' car hitting a mailbox and a tree. Burns, 64, was not wearing a seatbelt at the time of the crash, according to the Georgia State Patrol spokesman James Tallent. Burns was part of the band's original lineup, which formed in Jacksonville, Florida, in 1965, and included lead singer Ronnie Van Zant, guitarists Gary Rossington and Allen Collins, and bassist Larry Junstrom

Burns left the band before its third studio album, "Nuthin Fancy," in 1975, due to exhaustion from touring. He was not involved in the 1977 plane crash that killed three members of the band, including Van Zant. Lynyrd Skynyrd was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2006, and Gary Rossington remains the only original member still in the band. "I'm at a loss for words but I just remember Bob being a funny guy," Rossington said on the band's official Facebook page. The nuclear submarine that is currently being repaired at the Zvyozdochka shipyard in Russia has caught on fire, according to a law enforcement source speaking to Russia's state-run news agency ITAR-Tass. The submarine is in a dry dock and there is no ammunition on board. The fire began due to an issue with the rubber insulation between the submarine's light and pressure hull during welding work. The nuclear fuel from the sub's reactor has been unloaded, and there are no armaments, chemically active, dangerous substances, or fissionable materials on the submarine. The enterprise's personnel have evacuated the premises
The fire poses no threat to people or the shipyard.

The California Public Utilities Commission has ordered Pacific Gas & Electric (PG&E) to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for unsafe operation of its gas transmission system, following the 2010 pipeline rupture in San Bruno that killed eight people and injured over 50 others. Of this amount, $850 million will be allocated for gas transmission pipeline safety infrastructure improvements, and an additional $50 million for other remedies to enhance pipeline safety. Commission President Michael Picker expressed regret for the loss of life and destruction of homes, stating, "PG&E failed to uphold the public's trust, and the CPUC failed to keep vigilant." PG&E's CEO, Tony Earley, expressed deep sorrow for the event and the company's commitment to becoming the safest energy company in the US. Since the explosion, PG&E has paid over $500 million in claims to San Bruno victims, replaced over 800 miles of pipe, installed new gas leak technology, and implemented nine of 12 National Transportation Safety Board recommendations. The previous record penalty was a $146 million penalty against Southern California Edison Company in 2008 for falsifying customer and worker safety data.

in Tennessee on Saturday. The charges came after a state trooper stopped his private bus for not having the required U.S. Department of Transportation and International Fuel Tax Association stickers. During the subsequent search of the bus, the trooper found five rocks that tested positive for methamphetamine, a small amount of marijuana, drug paraphernalia, and several handguns, including a gold-plated .50-caliber Desert Eagle pistol, a .45-caliber Taurus pistol, and a .500 Smith & Wesson magnum, as well as 100 small Ziploc bags. Nelly and another passenger were taken to the Putnam County Jail. He later posted bond and was released

John Travolta, a high-profile Scientologist, has stated that he has not seen HBO's documentary "Going Clear: Scientology and the Prison of Belief" and has no intention of doing so. Travolta, who is premiering his new film "The Forger" in Clearwater, Florida, where the Church of Scientology is headquartered, believes the documentary is based on the experiences of disgruntled individuals and does not align with his positive experience with the organization. Travolta credits Scientology for helping him cope with the death of his son, Jett, and considers it a brilliant religion.

Based on the information provided, if a complete stranger asked me for $100 or offered me an apple without explanation during Jia Jiang's "100 Days of Rejection" project, I would follow Jiang's advice and not let the fear of rejection hold me back. I would engage with the stranger, be confident, and ask why they were asking for $100 or offering me an apple. I would also remember that rejection is more of a numbers game and that sometimes the most far-fetched idea gets a yes. Xanax, or alprazolam, is a benzodiazepine medication commonly used to alleviate symptoms of anxiety. It works by enhancing the effects of the neurotransmitter GABA in the brain, leading to a calming effect. Xanax is known for its rapid onset of action, making it effective for acute anxiety episodes. However, long-term use of Xanax can lead to tolerance development, requiring higher doses for the same effect and increasing the risk of dependence and withdrawal symptoms. For chronic anxiety management, other treatment options like therapy, lifestyle modifications, or alternative medications may be more sustainable.
. A holistic approach to anxiety management that incorporates various strategies is often the key to long-term well-being.

Rosalie Wahl was born Sara Rosalie Erwin in 1924 in Gordon, Kansas. Raised by her grandmother after the loss of her mother and other family members, Wahl developed a deep understanding of the struggles faced by the less fortunate. She studied sociology at the University of Kansas during World War II, where her passion for social justice was ignited.

After marrying Ross Wahl and relocating to Minnesota, Wahl spent several years as a homemaker and community volunteer before making a bold decision to attend William Mitchell College of Law in 1962. Despite being a mother of four, she excelled in her studies and later worked as an assistant public defender, building a strong reputation in criminal defense.

Her experience and dedication caught the attention of Governor Rudy Perpich, who appointed Wahl as the first female justice in the Minnesota Supreme Court in 1977. During her tenure, she chaired task forces on gender fairness and racial bias, leading to significant reforms that transformed the treatment of women and minorities within the court system. Her 549 written opinions displayed elegant clarity, emphasizing the implementation of legislative will and advocating for independent state constitutional standards.

Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton has filed a lawsuit against the Biden administration to prevent federal agents from cutting razor wire at the border. In the suit, Paxton alleges that the White House is undermining Texas' efforts to ensure security amidst record numbers of immigrants coming to the border. The state claims the right to construct border barriers to prevent illegal entry. However, environmental and immigration advocates have raised concerns about the razor wire's implications for the environment and human safety. The "concertina wire" barriers have reportedly injured migrants and required federal agents to cut them to free trapped migrants

Texas and the federal government have previously clashed over border barriers, with the Department of Justice suing Texas to remove buoys meant to block migrants from crossing the U.S.-Mexico border. The Biden administration claims the buoys pose threats to navigation and public safety, while Texas argues that the administration is breaking the law by trying to take down border barriers.
Luis Alfredo Jacobo, a California drug kingpin, has been sentenced to four life terms in federal prison for directing methamphetamine conspiracies in Oklahoma and Missouri
U.S. District Judge John C. Coughenour

including one life sentence for leading a continuing criminal enterprise and three additional life sentences for drug conspiracy. Jacobo was also sentenced to four years on 21 counts of unlawful use of a communication facility. From 2016 to 2021, Jacobo managed the distribution of methamphetamine, controlling pricing, methods of delivery, and payments, receiving bulk shipments of meth in California and overseeing their transportation to Oklahoma and Missouri. Three distinct drug conspiracies were operated by Jacobo's regional managers in Oklahoma, and evidence presented during the trial included communications and money exchanges related to the conspiracies, as well as the discovery of 231 pounds of methamphetamine and over $465,000 in a storage unit in Grove, Oklahoma. Jacobo's conviction was the result of Operation "Pullin Chains," a multi-agency effort led by the U.S. Attorney's Office and the Drug Enforcement Administration to target high-level drug traffickers and criminal organizations.
Laphonza Butler, the president of EMILY's List, has been appointed by California Governor Gavin Newsom to fill the vacant Senate seat left by the late Dianne Feinstein

Butler is the first Black lesbian to openly serve in the U.S. Senate. She is a Democratic strategist with a background in the labor movement and has previously served as an advisor to Kamala Harris' presidential campaign and as the executive director of the Service Employees International Union (SEIU) California. Prior to joining EMILY's List, Butler worked for Airbnb and was a partner at SCRB Strategies. Butler has been a member of the University of California Board of Regents and the board of directors for the Children’s Defense Fund and BLACK PAC. She grew up in Magnolia, MS, and attended Jackson State University. Butler's appointment is effective immediately, and she will serve in the Senate until the next regularly scheduled election in November 2024. Despite currently residing in Maryland, Butler owns a home in California and will switch her voter registration.

mechanics and technicians' entitlement to overtime wages depends on their place of work (auto dealer or repair shop), payment method (commission or not), hourly rate, commissions, and the specific state regulations.

applies to most shops unless they fit within specific exemptions. For instance, the 7th Circuit Court ruled in 311 F.3d 675 - Diaz v. Paul's Motor City, Inc. (2002) that certain automotive mechanics and technicians paid on a flat-rate or flag-rate are entitled to overtime under the FLSA.

Automotive technicians' average hourly wages vary depending on their geographic location. Technicians in Alaska have the highest average hourly wage at about $23.70 per hour or $49,400 per year. Technicians in Connecticut, Maryland, Massachusetts, and Washington D.C., have average hourly wages ranging from $20.50 to $21.50 per hour. A small number of automotive technicians work in aerospace products and parts manufacturing, which is the highest-paying opportunity for these workers, with an average hourly wage of about $32 per hour or $66,300 per year.

3. Thoroughly water the planting area.
4. Turn the soil over in your planned planting area with a hoe or tiller to a depth of 12 inches and break the dirt up finely. Remove any large rocks and other debris while hoeing.
5. Dig a trench about 4 inches wide and 6 to 8 inches deep. If planting more than one row, space them apart by about 24 to 36 inches.
6. Cut your potatoes into cubes, about 1 to 2 inches on each side, leaving a sprout on each cube.

7. Place the cubes into the bottom of the trench, separating each cube from the other by about 6 to 8 inches. Place the sprouted or eye side of the cubes facing up.
8. Cover the potatoes with a layer of compost and then replace the dirt on top

The Tennessee Flag was designed by Colonel LeRoy Reeves in 1905
officially adopted by the Tennessee State Legislature on April 17, 1905.

bordered in white. Within the circle are three white stars, each with five points, representing the three Grand Divisions of Tennessee: East, Middle, and West. The white stars also symbolize Tennessee's admission as the 16th state to the union.

The narrow vertical blue stripe on the flag's left side and thin white line on the inner side are not mentioned in the description provided by the flag's designer. The white stars on a field of blue represent the three additional stars on the American Flag. The large field of crimson is significant as it is one of the national colors of the US, representing readiness to shed blood during warfare. The final blue bar relieves the sameness of the crimson field and prevents the flag from showing too much crimson when hanging limp. In a 2001 poll conducted by the North American Vexillological Association, the flag of Tennessee was ranked 14th out of 72 flags of North America.
